summary: Simon Späti argues that traditional BI suffers from speed, transparency, and accessibility problems that data engineering approaches can solve. He presents twelve techniques including data lakes, Delta Lake for transactional processing, notebooks for democratizing access, Python/SQL pipelines, open-source tooling, incremental loading, and declarative programming. The post advocates for moving away from rigid data warehouse paradigms toward more flexible, transparent architectures that empower all data-savvy users. For organizations lacking resources, managed platforms like Ascend.io or Palantir Foundry offer these capabilities out-of-the-box.
key_points: Traditional BI is plagued by slow integration, lack of transparency, dependency on BI engineers, and difficulty handling unstructured data or real-time requirements
Data lakes with Delta Lake provide speed, schema flexibility, ACID transactions, and time-travel capabilities that solve many DWH limitations
Notebooks democratize data access but need governance to prevent code duplication and silo recreation
Incremental loading and idempotency enable near real-time analytics while eliminating lambda architecture complexity
Declarative pipelines (defining 'what' not 'how') represent the evolution from cron to modern orchestration tools
money_quotes: BI should produce a simple overview of your business, boost efficiency, and automate repetitive tasks across your organisation.
The BI department makes it more complicated than it requires to be.
Business keys are already understood by everyone, to some degree unique, holding the same value for the life of the data set, can be tracked and compared therefor all the way back.
The downside of notebooks is that your code will be duplicated and scattered around the place. A little bit like everyone having their own Excels.
Python is the tooling language of data engineers these days.
Be open to trying out along the way as you are not locked-in, you can always replace or adapt later on.
With the incremental approach, you switch from batch to event-driven. Your updates and inserts are independent, and you get autonomous batches.
Declarative programming is a paradigm that expresses the logic of a computation without describing its control flow.
key_insight: Modern data engineering techniques—data lakes, transactional processing, notebooks, and declarative pipelines—can transform opaque, slow BI systems into transparent, accessible platforms that empower all data-savvy users, not just BI specialists.
